activities_projects.title: The Global Timber Crisis and Imperial Extraction
activities_projects.objective: Deconstruct the link between European ecological exhaustion and Indian deforestation
steps: Investigate how the disappearance of oak forests in England in the 1820s created a 'timber supply problem' for the Royal Navy.
Analyze the role of search parties sent to India to explore forest resources as a direct response to this crisis.
Evaluate the 'Scientific Forestry' model as a tool for ensuring a perpetual supply of timber for colonial infrastructure.
Discuss the environmental cost of prioritizing 'teak and sal' over natural forest biodiversity.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the interconnectedness of global imperial needs and local ecological destruction.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of analysis regarding the global-to-local chain of resource extraction (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Legal Dispossession: The 1878 Forest Act
activities_projects.objective: Critique the use of legislation to criminalize traditional forest rights
steps: Deconstruct the three-tier classification of forests (Reserved, Protected, Village) established by the 1878 Act.
Analyze how the definition of 'theft' was expanded to include daily survival activities like grazing, collecting fuel-wood, and gathering fruit.
Explain the psychological and physical impact of the 'Forest Guard' as an agent of state surveillance and corruption.
Compare the loss of 'customary rights' in India with similar colonial legal frameworks in Southeast Asia.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how law serves as a primary instrument for economic and social dispossession.
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical evaluation of legislative impacts on rural sociology (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Sociological Impact: Shifting Cultivation vs. State Control
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the clash between indigenous land use and colonial commercial science
steps: Contrast the ecological logic of 'Swidden' agriculture (Shifting Cultivation) with the colonial obsession with 'Scientific Forestry'.
Evaluate the British arguments for banning shifting cultivation, focusing on revenue loss and timber protection.
Discuss the socio-economic displacement of communities forced to abandon 'penda' or 'bewar' practices.
Identify the global parallels of this conflict in the Amazon, Africa, and Southeast Asia.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Examines the delegitimization of indigenous knowledge by colonial administrative systems.
activities_projects.evaluation: Strength of arguments regarding the conflict of knowledge systems (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Resistance Case Study: The Bastar Rebellion of 1910
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the multi-causal nature of tribal resistance movements
steps: Analyze the converging triggers: 1905 forest reservation proposals, increased land rent, and forced labor (veth-begar).
Trace the strategic communication methods used by the Dhurwas, such as the distribution of 'lumps of earth and chilies'.
Critique the colonial military response and its impact on the Kanger forest communities.
Evaluate whether the temporary reduction in reserved areas (from 2/3rd to 1/2) constituted a victory for Gunda Dhur’s forces.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the tactical and political sophistication of indigenous resistance.
activities_projects.evaluation: Historical accuracy and critical assessment of resistance outcomes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Colonial Labor Systems: The Blandongdiensten in Java
activities_projects.objective: Compare Dutch and British strategies for extracting free forest labor
steps: Examine the Dutch system of providing land rent exemptions in exchange for free labor for timber transport.
Analyze the 'Saminist Movement' as a non-violent, ideological response to Dutch forest ownership claims.
Contrast the Saminist strategy of 'lying down on the land' with the militant rebellion in Bastar.
Discuss the role of the 'Kalangs' as a skilled but exploited labor class in Java’s teak forests.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the diversity of resistance strategies against colonial forest management.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of cross-cultural comparison between Indian and Indonesian resistance (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The War Imperative and Environmental Collapse
activities_projects.objective: Assess the impact of World War I and II on the sustainability of 'Scientific Forestry'
steps: Investigate how the 'Scorched Earth Policy' in Java led to the destruction of teak logs to prevent Japanese seizure.
Analyze why British working plans in India were abandoned during the World Wars to meet military demands.
Explain the ecological consequences of 'unrestricted felling' during wartime emergencies.
Compare the post-war forest recovery challenges in India and Indonesia.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects global military crises to immediate and long-term environmental degradation.
activities_projects.evaluation: Synthesis of war-time logistics and ecological consequences (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Commodification of the Wild: Hunting and Sport
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the hypocrisy of colonial conservation and hunting laws
steps: Deconstruct the shift where traditional tribal hunting was labeled 'poaching' while British hunting was 'sport'.
Evaluate the colonial reward system for killing 'dangerous' animals (tigers, wolves) as a 'civilizing mission'.
Research the statistics of animals killed between 1875 and 1925 (e.g., 80,000 tigers) and its impact on the food chain.
Discuss how Maharaja Sarguja's record of shooting 1,157 tigers represents the peak of this colonial hunting culture.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critiques the colonial binary of 'civilized sport' versus 'primitive poaching'.
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical analysis of environmental ethics and class privilege in hunting (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Industrial Labor: Tea Plantations and Forest Communities
activities_projects.objective: Trace the forced transition of forest dwellers into plantation laborers
steps: Identify the 'Criminal Tribes' Act and how it was used to control nomadic and forest communities.
Examine the recruitment of Santhals and Oraons for the tea plantations of Assam.
Analyze the low wages and 'restricted movement' policies that prevented workers from returning home.
Evaluate the plantation system as a 'new form of slavery' born from forest dispossession.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the link between forest laws and the creation of an unfree industrial labor force.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of sociological analysis regarding labor exploitation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Modern Environmentalism: Sarnas vs. State Forestry
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the survival of indigenous conservation models against modern state control
steps: Analyze the concept of 'Sacred Groves' (Sarnas, Deovarans) and their role in biodiversity preservation.
Critique the 'Joint Forest Management' model: Does it truly empower local communities?
Discuss the Forest Rights Act of 2006 as an attempt to rectify 'historical injustices'.
Argue whether the colonial legacy of 'Scientific Forestry' still dominates modern forest departments.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes historical legal structures with modern environmental justice movements.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the argument regarding historical continuity and change (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Economic History: Railways and the 'Sleeper' Demand
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the industrial scale of timber extraction for transportation
steps: Calculate the timber volume required for 35,000 miles of track by 1890, given each mile required 1,760 to 2,000 sleepers.
Explain how the 'contract system' for timber led to indiscriminate felling by private individuals.
Analyze the specific role of the Madras Presidency in providing over 35,000 trees annually for sleepers.
Describe the logistical challenges of transporting these logs from forests to railway construction sites.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Quantifies the industrial appetite of the colonial state and its ecological footprint.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of calculations and depth of logistical analysis (10 marks)
